The Creation of the World: Cronus overthrew his father Uranus and then did the same thing Uranus had done — suppressed his children to protect his own power. What does this cycle suggest about power? Why do you think powerful people so often become the thing they first fought against?

Rhea tricked Cronus by wrapping a stone in blankets — a small, simple deception that saved Zeus and changed everything. Why do you think the Greeks gave the victory in this moment to cleverness rather than strength? What does that say about what they valued?

The world began from chaos — complete formlessness — and produced order, beauty, and life. Do you think creation stories matter? What does the Greek creation story suggest about what the Greeks believed the world fundamentally was?
The Twelve Olympians: The Olympians were not always good. They were jealous, unfair, petty, and capable of great cruelty. Why do you think the Greeks worshipped gods who behaved badly? What might it mean to pray to a god you don't entirely trust?

Each Olympian ruled a different domain — sky, sea, crafts, harvest, love, war. What does it tell us about the Greeks that they believed every important part of life had its own divine protector with its own personality? How is that different from other ideas about god?
If you could ask one of the twelve Olympians for help with something in your life, who would you choose and why? What would you have to offer them in return?
Prometheus and the Gift of Fire: Prometheus knew what the punishment would be before he stole the fire, and he did it anyway. Do you think he was brave, reckless, or something else? Can you think of anyone in history — or in your own life — who accepted a serious personal cost to help others?
Zeus said humans weren't ready for fire. Prometheus disagreed. Who do you think was right — and does it matter? Is it ever right to give someone a powerful gift they weren't asked to receive?

The story says Prometheus never regretted what he did — even chained to a mountain with an eagle eating his liver every day. What would it take for you to hold that kind of conviction? Is there anything you believe in strongly enough that you'd pay that price for it?
Pandora's Box: Pandora was given curiosity as a gift and a sealed jar she was forbidden to open. Do you think Zeus set her up to fail on purpose? If so, what does that say about the story's real meaning — and who it's really about?

After everything terrible escaped from the jar, hope was still inside. The Greeks debated for centuries whether hope is a good thing or just another complication. What do you think? Is hope always helpful, or can it sometimes make things worse?

The story has often been used to blame Pandora for all the suffering in the world. But Epimetheus accepted Zeus's gift against his brother's warning, and Zeus designed the whole situation. Do you think Pandora deserves blame? What does it mean to hold someone responsible for a consequence they didn't intend?
Persephone and the Seasons: Zeus gave permission for Persephone to be taken without telling Demeter. He was Persephone's father. What does his choice tell you about him? How do you think a parent should balance their own decisions with the wellbeing of their child?

Demeter's response to grief was not rage but withdrawal — she just stopped working, and the world began to die. Why do you think the Greeks made the goddess of the harvest's grief so powerful? What does it suggest about what love looks like when it has real weight behind it?

The seasons exist, the Greeks said, because Persephone travels between two worlds every year. What do you think the seasons feel like to you — does the story of Demeter and Persephone change how you see them? What would it feel like to carry grief and joy on a cycle like that?
Orpheus and Eurydice: Orpheus turned around. Almost everyone who hears the story says they understand why. Why is that? What do you think he was feeling in those final steps, and what made the silence behind him so unbearable?

His music could stop rivers and move stones and make the Furies cry — but it couldn't bring her back. What does the story say about the limits of even the greatest talent? Are there things that skill and art simply cannot fix?

The story is told as a tragedy, but Orpheus did something no living person had ever done: he went to the underworld and nearly succeeded. How do you hold both things at once — the extraordinary effort and the failed outcome? Does trying matter even when you don't succeed?
Icarus and the Wings of Wax: Daedalus explained everything: middle height, not too low, not too high. Icarus understood, and flew higher anyway. Do you think Icarus made a choice, or was his behavior something almost inevitable given who he was and what he was experiencing? Is there a difference?

The story is usually told as a warning about not listening. But is it also about something else — about the fact that some experiences can't be understood until you're inside them? What does that mean for how we give advice to people we love?

Daedalus built the thing that killed his son, and then had to keep flying. He landed in Sicily alone. What do you think that felt like? What does the story say about the relationship between invention and consequence — about what happens when what you create goes beyond what you intended?
Midas and the Golden Touch: Midas asked for the golden touch immediately, without thinking it through. Dionysus warned him with an expression — he didn't say anything, but his face showed he knew what was coming. Why do you think the Greeks gave Dionysus that knowing look? What responsibility do we have when we can see someone is about to make a terrible mistake?

Midas gave away most of his gold after the wish was reversed, and was happier wandering in the woods listening to the wind. What changed for him? What did the golden touch teach him that he couldn't have learned any other way?

Midas's daughter was turned to gold and then restored. Some versions of the story say she was never quite the same afterward, even after she came back. What might it mean to be restored but changed? Can you think of something in your own life that was restored but different?
Perseus and Medusa: Perseus couldn't look at Medusa directly — he had to use the shield as a mirror. The Greeks used this image as a metaphor for facing terrible things: sometimes the only way to look at something dangerous is sideways. What do you think that means? Can you think of a situation where a direct approach might make things worse?

Two gods helped Perseus: Athena for practical reasons of her own, Hermes for reasons the story doesn't fully explain. Perseus asked for help and accepted it. How is asking for help part of what made him successful — not just the gifts he received, but the willingness to receive them?

Perseus freed Andromeda on his way home — she wasn't part of his quest at all, but he stopped and helped anyway. What does that detail add to the story? What does it say about what kind of person he was, separate from the famous quest he'd been sent on?
Theseus and the Minotaur: Theseus forgot to change the sails. His father had been watching every day. How do you hold that detail alongside everything else Theseus accomplished? Does one terrible mistake erase a great achievement — or do both things just have to exist together?

Ariadne gave Theseus the solution to the Labyrinth and left Crete to be with him, and he left her asleep on an island. The stories never fully explain why. What do you think happened — and what does the story say about how we treat people who help us when we needed them?

The Athenians had been sending young people to die in Crete for years because they felt they had no choice. Theseus changed that by deciding to go himself. When a situation seems impossible to change, what does it take to be the person who first says: I'll try?
Heracles and the Twelve Labors: The madness Hera sent was the cause of everything — Heracles didn't choose what he did in that fog. But he still accepted responsibility and did the twelve labors to make amends. Do you think he had to? Is it possible to owe penance for something you did while you didn't know yourself?

One of the labors — the Hydra — took two people. Heracles needed his nephew Iolaus. What does it say about Heracles that he accepted help when he needed it, rather than insisting on doing everything alone? How is knowing when to ask for help its own kind of strength?

Heracles was made a god after death — reconciled even with Hera, who had made his life miserable. What do you think the Greeks were saying about the end of that story? Is there something satisfying about that resolution, or does it feel too easy? What do you think he deserved?
Jason and the Golden Fleece: Medea gave up everything — her family, her home, her country — because she loved Jason and he swore an oath to her. He later broke that oath for political advantage. The gods noticed. What does the story say about the weight of a promise — especially one sworn by something larger than yourself?

Jason assembled the greatest heroes of the age: Heracles, Orpheus, Atalanta, fifty in total. But the quest was ultimately won by Medea, who wasn't a hero and wasn't invited — she simply saw a problem and solved it. What does that say about where the real power in the story lived?

Jason achieved everything he set out to achieve — the Fleece, the throne, fame — and still the story ends in wreckage. Do you think the story is saying that how you win matters as much as whether you win? What would a different version of Jason have looked like?
Atalanta and the Golden Apples: Atalanta could outrun anyone in the world, and she knew it. She paused for the apples — once, twice, three times — when she didn't have to. The Greeks said Aphrodite understood that Atalanta needed a reason to lose. What do you think that means? Did Atalanta want to be caught?

Atalanta was abandoned as a baby because her father wanted a son. She became the most famous female athlete in Greek mythology. The father who abandoned her then wanted her back when she was famous. What do you think about that arc? What does Atalanta owe her father, if anything?

Hippomenes forgot to thank Aphrodite, which was the mistake that undid everything good he'd won. Why do you think the Greeks considered gratitude so important — not just as good manners, but as something the gods actively enforced? What does that say about how they understood the relationship between luck, help, and acknowledgment?
Athena and Arachne: Arachne's tapestry was technically perfect. The only problem was what it depicted — the gods' bad behavior, shown truthfully and brilliantly. Was her punishment about her skill, her pride, or what she said? Can showing true things about powerful people be its own kind of dangerous act?

Athena destroyed the tapestry and struck Arachne, then transformed her into a spider rather than letting her die. The Greeks didn't let Athena off the hook for this — the story leaves room for judgment. What do you think of how Athena behaved? Can a goddess of wisdom act unwisely?

Arachne still weaves. Her name still means spider. What does it mean that her punishment preserved her craft — she is still doing the thing she was best at, forever? Is that a mercy, a cruelty, or something in between?
Echo and Narcissus: Echo could only repeat what others said — she could never begin a conversation, only respond. What would that be like? What parts of yourself would you lose if you couldn't speak your own thoughts first? What do you think it cost Echo most — the words she couldn't say, or the connection she couldn't make?

Narcissus died looking at his own reflection, reaching for something that could never reach back. The Greeks used his story to show a kind of love that is really just self-love — a mirror instead of a person. What is the difference between loving yourself and what Narcissus did? Where is the line?

The two stories are usually told together — Echo who could not speak for herself, Narcissus who could not see past himself. They met and neither could give the other what they needed. What do you think the Greeks were saying by pairing them? What would a healthier version of either story look like?
Odysseus and the Cyclops: Nobody was a perfect plan — and it almost worked completely. But Odysseus shouted his real name as they escaped because he wanted the Cyclops to know who had beaten him. The Greeks called this hubris. Have you ever done something like this — undone a success by needing credit for it? What drove him to do it?

Odysseus had every reason to kill Polyphemus when he had the chance — two of his men had been eaten. But he stopped himself because he needed the giant alive to move the boulder. How did that self-control save him? What does it take to hold back when you're furious and the opportunity is right there?

The curse Polyphemus called down on Odysseus — arrive home late, alone, to grief — came entirely true. Odysseus's pride cost him ten years and his entire crew. What does the story say about the relationship between intelligence and wisdom? Is it possible to be very clever and also foolish at the same time?
